review_comment: Wondering if it's worth to make this method `public` - Seems to be used just by `GenerateBase()` and testing, maybe we could test its behavior asserting on the result of `GenerateBase()`

I think this makes sense. This will force consumer to use the overload that requires specifying the `dataType` so the consumer is at least _forced_ to make a decision there (whether to pass `null` or some relevant `Type` instance). 👍  Great comments 👍 

EDIT: Removed the overload that does not accept `Type  dataType` parameter.
Personal opinion here 😛 : I'd be more inclined to just throw the exception and catch it if we want to ignore it internally, which will remove the need to return a `Tuple`

_Ironically_ -- it was initially written that way and later refactored following self review. A quick [google](https://www.google.com/search?q=exception+for+control+flow&rlz=1C1CHBD_enUS817US817&oq=exception+for+control+flow&aqs=chrome..69i57.3471j0j7&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8) will yield many examples as to why using exceptions for control flow is an anti-pattern and instead, exceptions should be reserved for truly exceptional cases that are unable to be reasonably handled by the executing code.

What would happen if we have `"SPY"` and `"SPY.MyCustomData"`, will the user be able to use `"SPY"` from the cache? Specifically thinking if it will cause this exception to be thrown
I think it would be a good idea to add an algorithm which showcases this issue, has a collision, and what is the expected behavior
Since `"SPY"` is a direct match, it will resolve properly. If there isn't already a unit test for this exact case, I'll be sure to add one.
